findings.description: Full card number (PAN) and CVV are written to the INFO log in plaintext, a textbook PCI-DSS violation since CVV must never be stored and PANs must never hit logs unmasked.
charge_order has no idempotency guard and never checks whether the order is already 'paid', so calling it twice happily charges the customer's card twice.
Monetary totals are computed and returned as raw floats (subtotal * 1.0825 with no final rounding), so accumulated float error turns into off-by-a-cent charges at int(round(total*100)).

Transaction IDs derive from Python's non-crypto, per-process-salted hash() truncated by % 10**10, so they are neither deterministic across restarts (the comment lies) nor collision-free, breaking reconciliation and idempotency.

Gateway charge (line 78) and DB status update (line 84) are a non-atomic dual write: if commit or the connection fails after a successful charge, the customer is charged but the order stays unpaid with no compensating refund.

monthly_revenue_report recomputes revenue as unit_price*quantity, ignoring the discount and tax that compute_order_total applied at charge time, so reported revenue will not match what was actually charged.
monthly_revenue_report issues one item query per paid order (classic N+1) instead of a single JOIN/aggregate, so the month-end report degrades linearly with order volume.
discount_pct is applied with no validation or clamp to [0,100], so 150 yields a negative line total and a negative discount silently inflates the charge.
charge_order never validates card_number/cvv (empty string makes card_number[-4:] yield '') nor re-checks the computed amount before hitting the gateway, leaning entirely on the gateway's own guard.
`order["discount_pct"] or 0.0` conflates a stored 0.0 discount with NULL, which is harmless arithmetically here but erases the null-vs-zero distinction.
SELECT * in the charge path couples the code to schema layout (fragile, silent breakage on column changes); not injection since the param is bound, but sloppy for a payments query.
api_key is stored as a cleartext instance attribute with no validation and is never actually used, a minor secret-handling smell.
The cvv parameter is threaded through PaymentProcessor.charge_order purely to be logged and forwarded; handling CVV in this persistence-adjacent layer at all is a design smell.
